title: Neuromyelitis Optica
authors: Bruce A. C. Cree, Stephen L. Hauser

items.title: Distinguishing Neuromyelitis Optica from Multiple Sclerosis
items.content: NMO is characterized by recurrent attacks of optic neuritis and myelitis, with a higher prevalence in women (9:1) and typically presenting in adulthood (mean age 40). It differs from MS in that NMO can have bilateral optic neuritis causing severe vision loss and longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis involving three or more vertebral segments. Progressive symptoms are rare in NMO.
keyPoints: NMO is more frequent in women than men (9:1)
Typically begins in adulthood with a mean age of onset at 40 years
Bilateral optic neuritis can cause severe vision loss, uncommon in MS
Myelitis in NMO is longitudinally extensive and involves three or more contiguous vertebral segments
Progressive symptoms are rare in NMO
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · Neuromyelitis Optica

items.title: Diagnostic Criteria for Neuromyelitis Optica Spectrum Disorder (NMOSD)
items.content: Diagnosis of NMOSD requires at least one core clinical characteristic and a positive test for AQP4-IgG using the best available detection method, with exclusion of alternative diagnoses. For NMOSD without AQP4-IgG or unknown status, two core clinical characteristics are needed, including optic neuritis, acute myelitis with LETM, or area postrema syndrome, along with negative AQP4-IgG testing and exclusion of other diagnoses.
keyPoints: NMOSD with AQP4-IgG requires at least one core clinical characteristic and a positive AQP4-IgG test
Exclusion of alternative diagnoses is necessary for NMOSD diagnosis
NMOSD without AQP4-IgG or unknown status requires two core clinical characteristics, including optic neuritis, acute myelitis with LETM, or area postrema syndrome
Negative AQP4-IgG test or testing unavailable is required for NMOSD without AQP4-IgG
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · Neuromyelitis Optica

items.title: Diagnosis of Neuromyelitis Optica (NMO) with MRI Requirements
items.content: Neuromyelitis Optica can be diagnosed using specific MRI criteria, especially when AQP4-IgG status is unknown or negative. Acute optic neuritis requires a T2-hyperintense lesion extending over more than half of the optic nerve length or involving the optic chiasm. Acute myelitis necessitates an intramedullary lesion spanning at least three contiguous segments, known as longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (LETM). Area postrema syndrome and acute brainstem syndrome require lesions in specific regions such as the dorsal medulla/area postrema and periependymal brainstem areas, respectively.
keyPoints: Acute optic neuritis: T2-hyperintense lesion extending >1/2 optic nerve length or involving optic chiasm
Acute myelitis: Intramedullary MRI lesion extending ≥3 contiguous segments (LETM)
Area postrema syndrome: Lesions in dorsal medulla/area postrema
Acute brainstem syndrome: Periependymal brainstem lesions
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · Core Clinical Characteristics

items.title: Pathophysiology and Diagnostic Markers of NMO
items.content: NMO is characterized by a distinctive astrocytopathy involving inflammation, loss of astrocytes, and absence of AQP4 staining. The disease is associated with autoantibodies against AQP4 found in ~90% of patients. These antibodies contribute to astrocyte injury through mechanisms like complement fixation and antibody-dependent cell-mediated cytotoxicity (ADCC). CSF findings often show pleocytosis greater than MS, with neutrophils and eosinophils present in acute cases, while oligoclonal bands are uncommon (<20%). Elevated IL-6 and GFAP levels during myelitis attacks indicate active inflammation and astrocyte injury.
keyPoints: AQP4 antibodies found in ~90% of NMO patients
CSF pleocytosis greater than MS, with neutrophils and eosinophils in acute cases
Oligoclonal bands uncommon (<20%)
Elevated IL-6 and GFAP during myelitis attacks
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · Core Clinical Characteristics

items.title: Prognosis and Relapse Risk in NMO
items.content: The presence of anti-AQP4 antibodies is highly specific for diagnosing NMO when accompanied by typical clinical presentations. Patients who are seropositive for AQP4 have a high risk of relapse, with more than half experiencing another attack within one year if left untreated.
keyPoints: Anti-AQP4 antibody presence is diagnostic in conjunction with clinical presentation
High relapse risk: >50% of anti-AQP4 seropositive patients will relapse within 1 year if untreated
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · Core Clinical Characteristics

items.subtopic: CLINICAL COURSE | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | ASSOCIATED CONDITIONS
items.title: NMO Recurrence and Prognosis: Importance of Early Treatment
items.content: Neuromyelitis Optica (NMO) is typically a recurrent disease, with only about 10% having a monophasic course. Untreated NMO leads to severe disability; one-third may experience respiratory failure from cervical myelitis, and within 8 years, 60% can become blind while over half suffer permanent limb paralysis. The introduction of therapies for acute attacks and relapse prevention has significantly improved long-term outcomes, increasing the 5-year survival rate from 68-75% to 91-98%.
keyPoints: NMO is recurrent in >90% of patients; monophasic course in <10%
Untreated NMO results in severe disability: respiratory failure in one-third, blindness in 60%, and limb paralysis in over half within 8 years
5-year survival rate increased from 68-75% (1999) to 91-98% (2017) due to improved therapies
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · CLINICAL COURSE | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | ASSOCIATED CONDITIONS

items.subtopic: CLINICAL COURSE | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | ASSOCIATED CONDITIONS
items.title: NMO Prevalence and Associated Autoimmune Conditions
items.content: The prevalence of NMO varies geographically, ranging from <1 to >4 per 100,000, with higher rates in individuals of Asian and African descent. Up to 40% of NMO patients have a systemic autoimmune disorder such as lupus or Sjögren's syndrome, distinguishing it from MS which rarely has comorbid autoimmune diseases other than hypothyroidism.
keyPoints: Prevalence of NMO ranges from <1 to >4 per 100,000
Higher prevalence in Asian and African populations; opticospinal MS subtype noted
Up to 40% of NMO patients have associated systemic autoimmune disorders
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · CLINICAL COURSE | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | ASSOCIATED CONDITIONS

items.title: Management of Acute NMO Attacks
items.content: Acute neuromyelitis optica (NMO) attacks are initially managed with high-dose glucocorticoids. If there is no response, plasma exchange is recommended.
keyPoints: High-dose glucocorticoids: methylprednisolone 1 g/d for 5–10 days followed by a prednisone taper
Plasma exchange: typically 5–7 exchanges of 1.5 plasma volumes/exchange if no response to glucocorticoids
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · ACUTE ATTACKS | PROPHYLAXIS AGAINST RELAPSES (part 1)

items.title: First-Line Prophylaxis in NMO
items.content: Inebilizumab and satralizumab are first-line treatments for preventing relapses in NMO. Inebilizumab significantly reduces the time to the first attack.
keyPoints: Inebilizumab: 77% reduction in time to first attack (hazard ratio, 0.23; p<.0001)
Satralizumab monotherapy: 74% risk reduction of attacks (p = .014)
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · ACUTE ATTACKS | PROPHYLAXIS AGAINST RELAPSES (part 1)

items.title: Second-Line Prophylaxis in NMO
items.content: Eculizumab is used as a second-line treatment for nonresponders to first-line therapies. It significantly reduces the risk of relapse and need for acute treatments.
keyPoints: Eculizumab: 94% relative risk reduction in time to first attack (hazard ratio, 0.058; p <.0001)
96% relative reduction in annualized attack rate
Dosing: 900 mg weekly for the first 4 weeks, then 1200 mg every 2 weeks
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · ACUTE ATTACKS | PROPHYLAXIS AGAINST RELAPSES (part 1)

items.title: Eculizumab and Meningococcal Vaccination Requirements in NMOSD
items.content: Eculizumab, a terminal complement inhibitor used for preventing relapses in neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (NMOSD), requires strict adherence to vaccination protocols due to its associated risk of life-threatening meningococcal infections. Patients must receive MenACWY and MenB vaccines prior to starting eculizumab therapy, with specific timing and revaccination schedules.
keyPoints: Eculizumab is available under a Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategy (REMS) due to its toxicity profile.
Patients must receive MenACWY vaccine in two doses at least 8 weeks apart and either MenB-4C or MenB-FHbp vaccines prior to eculizumab initiation, with specific timing requirements.
MenACWY requires revaccination every 5 years; MenB-4C/MenB-FHbp requires a booster 1 year after vaccination and then every 2-3 years during ongoing treatment.
Vaccination reduces but does not eliminate the risk of meningococcal infections.
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · PROPHYLAXIS AGAINST RELAPSES (part 2) | OTHER TREATMENTS

items.title: Ravulizumab as an Alternative to Eculizumab in NMOSD
items.content: Ravulizumab, a longer-acting terminal complement inhibitor developed using antibody recycling technology, offers less frequent dosing compared to eculizumab. It has demonstrated efficacy in preventing attacks over 50 weeks of observation and requires similar meningococcal vaccination protocols.
keyPoints: Ravulizumab is administered intravenously with a weight-based loading dose followed by maintenance dosing every 8 weeks.
In a single-arm study, ravulizumab-treated participants did not experience attacks during 50 weeks of observation.
Vaccination for meningococcal infections is required before starting ravulizumab therapy.
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · PROPHYLAXIS AGAINST RELAPSES (part 2) | OTHER TREATMENTS

items.title: Distinguishing MOGAD from NMO and MS: Clinical Features and MRI Findings
items.content: MOGAD can be differentiated from NMO and MS based on specific clinical features and MRI findings. Papillitis is a distinguishing feature, being common in MOGAD but rare in NMO and variable in MS. MRI typically shows longitudinally extensive optic neuritis in MOGAD, with brain lesions resembling those of NMO rather than the Dawson fingers typical for MS.
keyPoints: Papillitis seen by fundoscopy or orbital MRI is common in MOGAD, rare in NMO, and variable but usually unilateral in MS.
MRI findings: longitudinally extensive optic neuritis, brain lesions similar to NMO (fluffy areas of increased signal), uncommon MS-like lesions (Dawson fingers).
Spinal cord lesions can be longitudinally extensive or involve the conus medullaris.
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · MYELIN OLIGODENDROCYTE GLYCOPROTEIN ANTIBODY-ASSOCIATED DISEASE

items.title: Management and Treatment of Acute Episodes in MOGAD
items.content: Acute episodes of MOGAD are managed with high-dose glucocorticoids, followed by a prednisone taper. Plasmapheresis may be used similarly to NMO treatment. Brain lesions often respond rapidly to glucocorticoids and can resolve entirely. Some patients experience recurrence after discontinuation of prednisone, leading to potential glucocorticoid dependency.
keyPoints: Initial management with high-dose glucocorticoids followed by a prednisone taper.
Plasmapheresis may be used as in NMO treatment.
Brain lesions respond rapidly to glucocorticoids and may resolve entirely.
Recurrence can occur post-prednisone discontinuation, leading to potential glucocorticoid dependency.
Clinical trials with satralizumab (IL-6 receptor blocker) and rozanolixizumab underway.
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · MYELIN OLIGODENDROCYTE GLYCOPROTEIN ANTIBODY-ASSOCIATED DISEASE

items.subtopic: GLIAL FIBRILLARY ACIDIC PROTEIN (GFAP) AUTOIMMUNITY | ACUTE DISSEMINATED ENCEPHALOMYELITIS
items.title: Recognizing GFAP Autoimmunity: Clinical Presentation and Imaging Patterns
items.content: GFAP autoimmunity presents with symptoms including meningismus, encephalitis, myelitis, optic neuritis, and sometimes movement disorders. MRI findings are distinctive, showing gadolinium enhancement in GFAP-enriched CNS regions such as venous structures, leptomeninges, periependymal spinal cord, and a serpiginous pattern in brain parenchyma. These imaging patterns can mimic neurosarcoidosis.
keyPoints: Symptoms: meningismus, encephalitis, myelitis, optic neuritis, movement disorder
MRI findings: gadolinium enhancement in GFAP-enriched regions (venous structures, leptomeninges, periependymal spinal cord, serpiginous brain parenchyma)
Differential diagnosis includes neurosarcoidosis
Lymphocytic pleocytosis commonly present in CSF
GFAP antibodies detectable in serum or CSF
Paraneoplastic syndrome association (~25% cases), often with ovarian teratoma
Coexistence possible with anti-NMDAR encephalitis or NMO
T cells implicated in pathophysiology
Generally glucocorticoid responsive
Early recognition and intervention lead to favorable outcomes
Relapses occur in ~20% of patients, requiring immune suppression therapy
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · GLIAL FIBRILLARY ACIDIC PROTEIN (GFAP) AUTOIMMUNITY | ACUTE DISSEMINATED ENCEPHALOMYELITIS

items.subtopic: GLIAL FIBRILLARY ACIDIC PROTEIN (GFAP) AUTOIMMUNITY | ACUTE DISSEMINATED ENCEPHALOMYELITIS
items.title: Understanding ADEM: Epidemiology and Clinical Course
items.content: ADEM is characterized by a monophasic course, often following an antecedent infection or immunization. It predominantly affects children but can occur in adults, who may experience late relapses that qualify as multiple sclerosis (MS). Early recognition of ADEM is crucial for appropriate management.
keyPoints: Monophasic course
Often follows antecedent infection (postinfectious encephalomyelitis) or immunization (post-vaccinal encephalomyelitis)
More common in children than adults
Adult cases may have late relapses, qualifying as MS
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · GLIAL FIBRILLARY ACIDIC PROTEIN (GFAP) AUTOIMMUNITY | ACUTE DISSEMINATED ENCEPHALOMYELITIS

items.question: Which antibody is primarily associated with Neuromyelitis Optica (NMO)?
items.explanation: The primary antibody associated with NMO is AQP4-IgG.
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · Neuromyelitis Optica

items.question: What is a distinguishing feature of myelitis in Neuromyelitis Optica compared to Multiple Sclerosis?
options: Involvement usually affects fewer than three vertebral segments
items.explanation: In NMO, myelitis can be severe and longitudinally extensive involving three or more contiguous vertebral segments.
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · Neuromyelitis Optica

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a core clinical characteristic required for diagnosing NMOSD without AQP4-IgG?
items.explanation: Progressive symptoms are not a core clinical characteristic for diagnosing NMOSD without AQP4-IgG.
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · Neuromyelitis Optica

items.question: Which of the following is required for diagnosing acute myelitis in NMOSD without AQP4-IgG?
options: Associated intramedullary MRI lesion extending ≥3 contiguous segments (LETM)
Presence of oligoclonal bands in CSF
items.explanation: Acute myelitis diagnosis requires an associated intramedullary MRI lesion extending ≥3 contiguous segments (LETM).
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · Core Clinical Characteristics

items.question: What is a typical CSF finding in NMO compared to MS?
options: Pleocytosis with neutrophils and eosinophils present
items.explanation: CSF findings in NMO include pleocytosis greater than that observed in MS, with neutrophils and eosinophils present.
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · Core Clinical Characteristics

items.question: Which brainstem syndrome is associated with periependymal lesions in NMOSD?
items.explanation: Acute brainstem syndrome requires periependymal brainstem lesions.
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · Core Clinical Characteristics

items.question: What is the role of AQP4 antibodies in NMO pathogenesis?
options: They contribute to astrocyte injury via complement fixation and ADCC
They prevent inflammation by inhibiting T lymphocytes
items.explanation: AQP4 antibodies are pathogenic because they contribute to astrocyte injury via complement fixation and antibody-dependent cell-mediated cytotoxicity (ADCC).
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · Core Clinical Characteristics

items.subtopic: CLINICAL COURSE | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | ASSOCIATED CONDITIONS
items.question: Which of the following statements is true regarding the clinical course of NMO?
options: NMO typically has a monophasic course in more than 50% of patients.
Untreated NMO usually results in mild disability over time.
The long-term survival rate for NMO has improved due to better therapies and diagnosis.
Respiratory failure from cervical myelitis is uncommon in untreated NMO.
items.explanation: Observational data suggest that the long-term course of NMO has been substantially improved since the development of therapies to treat acute attacks and prevent relapses, increasing survival rates.
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · CLINICAL COURSE | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | ASSOCIATED CONDITIONS

items.subtopic: CLINICAL COURSE | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | ASSOCIATED CONDITIONS
items.question: Which populations are disproportionately affected by NMO?
options: Individuals of Asian and African origin.
NMO affects all ethnic backgrounds equally.
Only individuals with a family history of autoimmune diseases.
items.explanation: Although NMO can occur in people of any ethnic background, individuals of Asian and African origin are disproportionately affected.
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · CLINICAL COURSE | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | ASSOCIATED CONDITIONS

items.subtopic: CLINICAL COURSE | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | ASSOCIATED CONDITIONS
items.question: Which autoimmune disorders are commonly associated with NMO?
options: Rheumatoid arthritis, Crohn's disease, and ulcerative colitis.
Systemic lupus erythematosus, Sjögren's syndrome, and myasthenia gravis.
Type 1 diabetes mellitus and Addison's disease.
items.explanation: Up to 40% of NMO patients have a systemic autoimmune disorder such as systemic lupus erythematosus, Sjögren's syndrome, or myasthenia gravis.
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · CLINICAL COURSE | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | ASSOCIATED CONDITIONS

items.question: Which monoclonal antibody medication is used as a first-line treatment for NMO attack prevention?
items.explanation: Inebilizumab or satralizumab are generally used as first-line treatments for NMO attack prevention.
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · ACUTE ATTACKS | PROPHYLAXIS AGAINST RELAPSES (part 1)

items.question: What is the initial dosing schedule for inebilizumab?
options: 300 mg IV followed by a second dose after 2 weeks and then every 6 months
items.explanation: Inebilizumab is dosed as an initial 300-mg IV infusion followed 2 weeks later by a second 300-mg IV infusion, with subsequent doses of 300-mg infusions every 6 months thereafter.
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · ACUTE ATTACKS | PROPHYLAXIS AGAINST RELAPSES (part 1)

items.question: Which medication is associated with a dose-dependent decline in serum IgG levels?
items.explanation: Inebilizumab is associated with a dose-dependent decline in serum IgG levels.
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · ACUTE ATTACKS | PROPHYLAXIS AGAINST RELAPSES (part 1)

items.question: What is the primary mechanism of action for eculizumab?
options: Depletes B cells by targeting CD19
Inhibits cleavage of complement protein C5
items.explanation: Eculizumab binds with high affinity to the complement protein C5, inhibiting its cleavage into C5a and C5b.
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · ACUTE ATTACKS | PROPHYLAXIS AGAINST RELAPSES (part 1)

items.question: Which vaccine regimen is required for eculizumab-treated patients to prevent meningococcal infections?
options: MenACWY two doses at least 8 weeks apart, followed by MenB-4C or MenB-FHbp with appropriate dosing intervals
MenACWY single dose and MenB-4C booster every year
MenACWY three doses over 6 months plus MenB-FHbp two doses at least 1 month apart
No specific vaccine regimen is required
items.explanation: Eculizumab-treated patients must receive MenACWY and either MenB-4C or MenB-FHbp with specified dosing intervals.
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · PROPHYLAXIS AGAINST RELAPSES (part 2) | OTHER TREATMENTS

items.question: What is the administration frequency of ravulizumab after the initial loading dose?
items.explanation: Ravulizumab is administered every 8 weeks after the initial loading dose.
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · PROPHYLAXIS AGAINST RELAPSES (part 2) | OTHER TREATMENTS

items.question: Which of the following statements about interferon beta in NMO treatment is correct?
options: Interferon beta is effective and reduces relapse rates.
Interferon beta has no effect on NMO but may increase relapse risk.
Interferon beta is used as a first-line therapy for NMOSD.
Interferon beta is recommended in combination with eculizumab.
items.explanation: Interferon beta appears to be ineffective and may increase the risk of NMO relapses.
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · PROPHYLAXIS AGAINST RELAPSES (part 2) | OTHER TREATMENTS

items.question: Which clinical feature can help distinguish optic neuritis (ON) associated with MOG antibody-associated disease (MOGAD) from NMO or MS?
options: Presence of papillitis seen by fundoscopy or orbital MRI
Finger-like lesions oriented perpendicular to the ventricular surface (Dawson fingers)
items.explanation: Papillitis is common in MOGAD, rare in NMO, and variable but usually unilateral in MS.
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · MYELIN OLIGODENDROCYTE GLYCOPROTEIN ANTIBODY-ASSOCIATED DISEASE

items.question: Which of the following is uncommon in MRI lesions typical for MOG antibody-associated disease (MOGAD)?
options: Fluffy areas of increased signal change in white or gray matter structures
Finger-like lesions oriented perpendicular to the ventricular surface (Dawson fingers)
items.explanation: MRI lesions typical for MS, including Dawson fingers and T1 hypointense lesions, are uncommon in MOGAD.
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · MYELIN OLIGODENDROCYTE GLYCOPROTEIN ANTIBODY-ASSOCIATED DISEASE

items.question: What is the typical management for acute episodes of MOG antibody-associated disease (MOGAD)?
options: High-dose glucocorticoids followed by a prednisone taper and sometimes plasmapheresis
items.explanation: Acute episodes are managed with high-dose glucocorticoids followed by a prednisone taper and sometimes plasmapheresis.
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · MYELIN OLIGODENDROCYTE GLYCOPROTEIN ANTIBODY-ASSOCIATED DISEASE

items.subtopic: GLIAL FIBRILLARY ACIDIC PROTEIN (GFAP) AUTOIMMUNITY | ACUTE DISSEMINATED ENCEPHALOMYELITIS
items.question: Which of the following is a common clinical feature associated with GFAP autoimmunity?
items.explanation: Fever and headache are commonly present in cases of GFAP autoimmunity.
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · GLIAL FIBRILLARY ACIDIC PROTEIN (GFAP) AUTOIMMUNITY | ACUTE DISSEMINATED ENCEPHALOMYELITIS

items.subtopic: GLIAL FIBRILLARY ACIDIC PROTEIN (GFAP) AUTOIMMUNITY | ACUTE DISSEMINATED ENCEPHALOMYELITIS
items.question: Which CNS regions show characteristic patterns of gadolinium enhancement in GFAP autoimmunity?
options: Venous structures in a periventricular radial orientation, the leptomeninges, and the periependymal spinal cord
items.explanation: MRI shows gadolinium enhancement in GFAP-enriched CNS regions including venous structures, leptomeninges, and periependymal spinal cord.
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · GLIAL FIBRILLARY ACIDIC PROTEIN (GFAP) AUTOIMMUNITY | ACUTE DISSEMINATED ENCEPHALOMYELITIS

items.subtopic: GLIAL FIBRILLARY ACIDIC PROTEIN (GFAP) AUTOIMMUNITY | ACUTE DISSEMINATED ENCEPHALOMYELITIS
items.question: What percentage of GFAP autoimmunity cases are found as a paraneoplastic syndrome?
items.explanation: GFAP autoimmunity is found as a paraneoplastic syndrome in ~25% of cases.
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · GLIAL FIBRILLARY ACIDIC PROTEIN (GFAP) AUTOIMMUNITY | ACUTE DISSEMINATED ENCEPHALOMYELITIS

items.explanation: NMO is more frequent in women, with a ratio of about 9:1.

items.explanation: NMO typically begins in adulthood with a 40-year mean age of onset.

items.explanation: Oligoclonal bands are uncommon, occurring in <20% of NMO patients.

items.statement: MS is more common than NMO among white populations.
items.explanation: Among white populations, MS is far more common than NMO.

items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · CLINICAL COURSE | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | ASSOCIATED CONDITIONS

items.statement: Satralizumab has a clinically meaningful impact on AQP4-seronegative participants.
items.explanation: There was no clinically meaningful impact of satalizumab in seronegative participants.

items.statement: Plasma exchange is used for acute NMO attacks that respond to glucocorticoids.

items.statement: Ravulizumab requires less frequent administration than eculizumab due to its longer half-life.

items.statement: Mycophenolate mofetil was a disease-modifying therapy approved specifically for NMO.
items.explanation: Mycophenolate mofetil was used empirically prior to the approval of specific therapies like eculizumab.

items.statement: Oligoclonal bands are present in approximately 50% of MOGAD cases.

items.assertion: NMO is characterized by distinctive astrocytopathy.
items.reason: Pathologic studies show loss of AQP4 associated with antibody and complement deposition and cytolysis of astrocytes within NMO lesions.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · Core Clinical Characteristics

items.assertion: Anti-AQP4 antibodies are non-specific to NMOSD.
items.reason: AQP4 is localized to astrocytes in close apposition to endothelial surfaces.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; anti-AQP4 antibodies are highly specific to NMOSD. The reason is true but does not relate to the assertion.
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · Core Clinical Characteristics

items.assertion: Inebilizumab reduces the time to the first attack by 77% compared to placebo.
items.reason: Inebilizumab binds to CD19 and depletes a wide range of B cells.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is correct as it explains how inebilizumab works.
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · ACUTE ATTACKS | PROPHYLAXIS AGAINST RELAPSES (part 1)

items.assertion: Eculizumab is used as monotherapy for NMO.
items.reason: Eculizumab was investigated as an add-on therapy in AQP4-seropositive NMO.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; eculizumab is not primarily used as monotherapy but rather as an add-on therapy.
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · ACUTE ATTACKS | PROPHYLAXIS AGAINST RELAPSES (part 1)

items.assertion: MOGAD lesions on MRI are typically longitudinally extensive.
items.reason: MRI lesions typical for MS, including Dawson fingers and T1 hypointense lesions, are uncommon in MOGAD.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why MOGAD lesions are longitudinally extensive.
items.reference: Neuromyelitis Optica · MYELIN OLIGODENDROCYTE GLYCOPROTEIN ANTIBODY-ASSOCIATED DISEASE
